edibility: DEADLY — responsible for ~90% of fatal mushroom poisonings worldwide; cooking does NOT destroy amatoxins; never eaten. The lethal dose is extremely small, with just half a cap capable of killing an adult.
taste: Reportedly pleasant taste — described as 'good' by survivors, which contributes to fatal misidentifications. This deceptive palatability makes it particularly dangerous as victims may consume a lethal dose before any warning signs appear.
search: Late summer/fall, especially under oaks (especially in California, US East coast, Pacific Northwest). Characterized by greenish-olive cap, white gills, white sac (volva) at base, and white ring. Dangerous look-alikes include edible paddy straw mushrooms (Volvariella) and grisettes, so extreme caution is needed for identification.

toxicity.symptoms: Phase 1 (6-24h): violent gastrointestinal distress including severe abdominal pain, vomiting, and diarrhea; Phase 2 (24-72h): false recovery with apparent improvement masking ongoing organ damage; Phase 3 (3-6d): hepatic and renal failure, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, and shock
toxicity.treatmentWindow: First 24-48 hours critical for activated charcoal, IV silibinin, and N-acetylcysteine; after significant liver damage, transplant may be the only option
toxicity.antidote: Silibinin (IV milk thistle extract) + N-acetylcysteine + activated charcoal + supportive care; possible liver transplant for acute liver failure
